summary: Re-verify all packet-012 audit findings against current code; produce scope-locked fix specs per cluster

findings.title: Non-zero CLI lineage exits / timeouts counted as successful fan-out results
findings.currentLocation: fanout-run.cjs:344-363 (worker), fanout-run.cjs:376 (exit code from summary); fanout-pool.cjs:76-119 (settleItem) + 207-220 (buildPoolSummary)
findings.evidence: The worker at fanout-run.cjs:344 runs spawnSync and at line 362-363 returns `{ label, exitCode, timedOut: result.signal === 'SIGTERM', salvage }` WITHOUT ever throwing on a non-zero exitCode or timeout. settleItem (fanout-pool.cjs:85-99) marks a result `status:'fulfilled'` for ANY worker that does not throw. buildPoolSummary (fanout-pool.cjs:207-219) computes `succeeded = results.filter(r => r.status === 'fulfilled')` and `failed = total - succeeded`, never inspecting `output.exitCode` or `output.timedOut`. Therefore a lineage whose CLI exits non-zero or times out is counted as succeeded. fanout-run.cjs:376 then derives the process exit code purely from `summary.all_failed`/`summary.failed`, so a failed/timed-out lineage yields exit 0 and status 'ok'. The existing test even concedes the gap at tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts:143 ('stub exits 0 but pool records non-zero — acceptable'); no test asserts that a non-zero exit becomes a failure.
findings.fix: In the fanout-run.cjs worker (lines 359-363), treat a non-zero exitCode or a timeout as a failure so settleItem records it as rejected. Minimal change: after computing `const exitCode = result.status ?? (result.error ? 1 : 0);` and `const timedOut = result.signal === 'SIGTERM';`, if `timedOut || exitCode !== 0` then `throw` an Error carrying the label/exitCode/timedOut/salvage context (e.g. `const e = new Error(...); e.exitCode = exitCode; e.timedOut = timedOut; e.salvage = salvage; throw e;`) instead of returning the success object. This routes through settleItem's catch (fanout-pool.cjs:100-118) and is counted in summary.failed/all_failed, which then propagates to the exit code at fanout-run.cjs:376. Do NOT alter buildPoolSummary's fulfilled/rejected contract (other callers, incl. council, rely on it). Keep the salvage write (lines 354-360) BEFORE the throw so stdout/iteration recovery still runs.
findings.verification: Add a regression case in .opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts using a stub binary that exits non-zero (the existing writeStubBinary helper plus an exit-code variant), asserting result.exitCode === 2 (or 3 when all fail) and that orchestration-summary.json has failed>=1. Run: cd .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server && npx vitest run ../../deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts ../../deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/fanout-pool.vitest.ts
findings.risk: settleItem/buildPoolSummary are SHARED with the council dispatcher (lib/council/multi-seat-dispatch.cjs) and tested directly by tests/unit/fanout-pool.vitest.ts — do NOT change pool semantics; confine the fix to the worker so the pool contract is preserved. The throw must occur AFTER the salvage sweep (fanout-run.cjs:354-360) so iteration recovery is not lost. Coupling with A4 (same worker/buildLineageCommand path) and A2 (same spawnSync site) — sequence A1 first since it changes the worker's return/throw behavior the other two touch nearby.

findings.title: spawnSync serializes lineages despite the concurrency cap (concurrency=N still runs one at a time)
findings.currentLocation: fanout-run.cjs:344 (spawnSync inside the async worker); fanout-pool.cjs:174-194 (pump loop)
findings.evidence: fanout-pool.cjs:174-194 (pump) launches up to `concurrency` workers via `settleItem(...).then/.catch/.finally`, expecting each worker to yield to the event loop so K run concurrently. But the worker at fanout-run.cjs:344 calls the BLOCKING `spawnSync(command, cmdArgs, {... timeout: timeoutMs ...})`. Because the worker is `async` but its body is synchronous up to and through spawnSync, the first worker blocks the single Node thread until its child process exits; only then does control return to pump to start the next. Net effect: the pool's K-in-flight cap is defeated — lineages execute serially regardless of fanoutConfig.concurrency (default 2, schema executor-config.ts:306).
findings.fix: Replace the blocking spawnSync at fanout-run.cjs:344-351 with an async non-blocking spawn that the worker awaits (e.g. `child_process.spawn` wrapped in a Promise that resolves `{ status, signal, stdout, error }`, honoring the same `timeout`, `env`, `maxBuffer`, `cwd`, and stdin `input` options). The async import at the top (line 21 currently destructures only `spawnSync`) must also bring in `spawn`. Preserve the exact result shape consumed downstream: `result.status`, `result.signal` (SIGTERM on timeout), `result.stdout`, `result.error`. Keep maxBuffer 20MB and the timeout-kill semantics (kill the child on timeout and set signal to SIGTERM so the A1 timedOut check still fires).
findings.verification: Extend tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts with a timing assertion: two stub binaries that each sleep ~1s with concurrency:2 should complete in ~1s (parallel) not ~2s (serial). Run: cd .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server && npx vitest run ../../deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts
findings.risk: Highest-coupling fix in the cluster: it rewrites the same spawn site A1 and A4 sit on, and changes I/O + timeout handling. The stdin `input` path (fanout-run.cjs:350, used by cli-codex/cli-devin/cli-opencode) and SIGTERM-on-timeout signal must be preserved exactly or A1's timedOut detection breaks. Sequence AFTER A1 (so A1's throw-on-failure logic is in place) and coordinate with A4 since both edit buildLineageCommand-adjacent code. The TSX bootstrap (fanout-run.cjs:52-66) also uses spawnSync — leave it untouched; only the worker's spawnSync changes.

findings.title: Per-lineage `iterations` only sizes the timeout, never caps the loop (runs to convergence)
findings.currentLocation: executor-config.ts:299 (schema field + doc comment 290-292); fanout-run.cjs:122-147 (buildLoopPrompt), fanout-run.cjs:154-158 (computeLineageTimeoutMs)
findings.evidence: lineageExecutorSchema declares `iterations: z.number().int().positive().nullable().default(null)` (executor-config.ts:299) documented as 'per-lineage max-iterations override' (line 292). But the only consumer of `lineage.iterations` is computeLineageTimeoutMs (fanout-run.cjs:154-157: `const iters = lineage.iterations || 12; ... return Math.min(iters * perIterSecs * 2 * 1000, 4h)`) — it merely sizes the subprocess timeout. buildLoopPrompt (fanout-run.cjs:128-146) hard-codes 'execute the loop ... to convergence' and 'Run phase_init, phase_main_loop (to convergence), and phase_synthesis' (lines 131,143) and NEVER passes a max_iterations / iteration cap to the subprocess. So `iterations` does not cap the loop; the lineage runs until convergence and `iterations` only controls how long before the timeout kills it.
findings.fix: Thread `lineage.iterations` into the loop prompt as an explicit cap. In buildLoopPrompt (fanout-run.cjs:122-147), when `lineage.iterations` is a positive number, add a parameter line to the prompt params block (alongside `loop_type: ${loopType}` at line 136), e.g. `  config.max_iterations: ${lineage.iterations}` and change the convergence instruction (lines 131/143) to 'to convergence OR config.max_iterations, whichever comes first'. This makes the cap honored by the subprocess loop. No schema change needed; the field already exists. Confirm the downstream deep-research/deep-review loop reads a max_iterations/config override (the prompt already relies on config.fanout_lineage_artifact_dir override at line 133, same mechanism).
findings.verification: Add a unit assertion on the exported/exercised buildLoopPrompt output (if not exported, assert via the prompt captured by a stub binary that echoes argv/stdin in tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts) that the prompt contains the iteration cap when lineage.iterations is set and omits it when null. Run: cd .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server && npx vitest run ../../deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts
findings.risk: Behavioral change to what the subprocess does (caps a previously-unbounded loop). Must verify the deep-research/deep-review loop actually consumes a `config.max_iterations`-style override or the prompt line is inert. Low coupling with A1/A2 (different function, buildLoopPrompt vs worker), but shares fanout-run.cjs so coordinate edits to avoid conflicts. Keep the timeout sizing in computeLineageTimeoutMs unchanged — it remains a safety backstop.

findings.title: Codex dispatch emits service_tier=default, outside the validated enum (priority/standard/fast)
findings.evidence: buildLineageCommand for cli-codex (fanout-run.cjs:167-186) passes `'-c', \`service_tier=${lineage.serviceTier || 'default'}\`` at line 176-177. When lineage.serviceTier is null/undefined the fallback is the literal string 'default', which is NOT a member of SERVICE_TIERS (`['priority','standard','fast']`, executor-config.ts:13). The validator (parseExecutorConfig, executor-config.ts:180-258) only validates serviceTier when it is non-null via the zod enum at line 27 (`z.enum(SERVICE_TIERS).nullable().default(null)`), so a null serviceTier passes validation and then this code injects the unvalidated 'default' string straight onto the Codex CLI `-c service_tier=` flag.
findings.fix: Make the cli-codex branch omit the `-c service_tier=...` pair entirely when lineage.serviceTier is falsy, instead of substituting 'default'. Build the args array conditionally: include `['-c', \`service_tier=${lineage.serviceTier}\`]` only when `lineage.serviceTier` is set; otherwise drop both tokens. This keeps every emitted service_tier value inside the validated enum and lets the Codex CLI apply its own default. Confine the change to the cli-codex block (fanout-run.cjs:167-186); leave model/reasoningEffort/sandbox handling untouched.
findings.verification: Add/extend a case in tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts (or tests/unit/cli-matrix.vitest.ts which already exercises per-kind arg shapes) asserting that buildLineageCommand for cli-codex with serviceTier=null produces args WITHOUT 'service_tier=default', and with serviceTier='standard' includes 'service_tier=standard'. Run: cd .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server && npx vitest run ../../deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/cli-matrix.vitest.ts ../../deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/fanout-run.vitest.ts
findings.risk: Shares the buildLineageCommand function and the same spawn path as A1/A2 — sequence after/with them to avoid edit conflicts. Low semantic risk: only changes the codex arg list when serviceTier is unset. Verify the Codex CLI tolerates absence of the -c service_tier flag (it should fall back to its account default).

findings.title: Sandbox default is workspace-write even for read-only review lineages (write scope is prompt-only)
findings.evidence: normalizeSandboxMode (executor-config.ts:83-85) returns `mode ?? 'workspace-write'` — so any lineage with sandboxMode=null (the schema default, line 28) resolves to a sandbox that PERMITS workspace writes. For a review loop, the only thing constraining writes to the lineage dir is the prompt text in buildLoopPrompt (fanout-run.cjs:143: 'Do not touch any path outside ${lineageDir}') — a soft, model-honored instruction, not an enforced sandbox boundary. resolveCodexSandboxMode (line 129-131), resolveGeminiSandboxMode (139-141), resolveClaudePermissionMode (149-158), and resolveDevinPermissionMode (166-168) all route through this same default, so unless the caller explicitly sets read-only, every executor gets write-capable sandbox/permission. The loopType ('research' vs 'review') is not consulted when choosing the default.
findings.fix: Scope-locked option: leave normalizeSandboxMode's general default as-is (other callers depend on workspace-write) and instead make the review fan-out path pass an explicit read-only sandboxMode. Minimal: in fanout-run.cjs main()/worker (around the resolve* calls at lines 320-326), when `loopType === 'review'` and `lineage.sandboxMode` is null, resolve sandbox/permission as if 'read-only' (pass 'read-only' into the resolve* helpers for that case). This makes review lineages read-only by default without changing the shared normalize default that research/native rely on. Do NOT broaden scope to rework the schema default.
findings.verification: Add a unit case (tests/unit/cli-matrix.vitest.ts or fanout-run.vitest.ts) asserting that for loopType 'review' with sandboxMode null, the resolved codex sandbox is 'read-only' and the claude permission is 'plan'; and that 'research' still resolves to workspace-write/acceptEdits. Run: cd .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server && npx vitest run ../../deep-loop-runtime/tests/unit/cli-matrix.vitest.ts
findings.risk: P2 — policy/hardening change. Touches the same fanout-run.cjs resolve* block as A1/A2/A4; sequence to avoid conflicts. Verify read-only mode does not break review lineages that legitimately need to write iteration artifacts INTO lineageDir — if review subprocesses write their own iteration files, read-only would block them, so confirm the artifact-write path before defaulting to read-only (may need workspace-write scoped to lineageDir rather than full read-only). This nuance must be settled before implementing.

result.notes:
All 7 findings CONFIRMED against the current working tree; none were already fixed by the ~26 uncommitted changes. Cited line numbers were largely accurate with minor drift (A1 worker now at 344-363; A3 schema field at executor-config.ts:299 with doc comment 290-292; A4 at fanout-run.cjs:176-177).

Headline P0 (A1) trace, exactly as requested: spawnSync result -> worker returns {exitCode, timedOut} WITHOUT throwing (fanout-run.cjs:362-363) -> settleItem marks it status:'fulfilled' because no throw (fanout-pool.cjs:85-99) -> buildPoolSummary counts succeeded purely by status==='fulfilled' and never reads output.exitCode/output.timedOut (fanout-pool.cjs:207-219) -> main() derives the process exit code from summary.all_failed/summary.failed only (fanout-run.cjs:376). Net: a non-zero exit, a missing FANOUT_LINEAGE_COMPLETE sentinel, or a SIGTERM timeout is reported as success (exit 0, status 'ok'). The fix MUST live in the worker (throw on failure), NOT in buildPoolSummary, because the pool primitive is shared with the council dispatcher and is directly contract-tested by tests/unit/fanout-pool.vitest.ts.

Sequencing within the cluster: A1, A2, A4, A5 all edit the SAME spawn/worker/buildLineageCommand region of fanout-run.cjs (lines ~320-363) — implement in order A1 -> A2 -> A4 -> A5 to avoid edit collisions, since A2 (async spawn rewrite) is the most invasive and depends on A1's throw-on-failure being in place to count timeouts/non-zero as failures. A3 is isolated (buildLoopPrompt). A6 (SKILL.md) and A7 (comments) are doc/comment-only and independent.

Ownership note honored: context-server.ts (cluster D) was NOT touched. For A7, the cluster owns ONLY the comment fixes in system-code-graph/mcp_server/**; the actual logic there is out of scope.

Two items need a decision before coding, flagged in their risk fields: (A5) whether review lineages must write iteration artifacts into lineageDir — if so, full read-only is wrong and a lineageDir-scoped workspace-write is needed instead; (A7) whether the PHASE-00x SLOT placeholders should be deleted or merely neutralized given future 027/002-004 phase work may rely on them as anchors. Also for A7, fanout-pool.cjs has a SECOND 'packet-122' occurrence at line 232 (beyond the cited line 8) that must also be fixed to avoid a PARTIAL.

findings.title: Community-search fallback bypasses governed retrieval scope (fetches member rows WHERE id IN (...) with no tenant/user/agent filter)
findings.evidence: The fallback at lines 1000-1011 calls searchCommunities() then runs `SELECT id, title, similarity, content, file_path, anchor_id, document_type, importance_tier, context_type, quality_score, created_at FROM memory_index WHERE id IN (${placeholders})` and merges the rows directly into resultsForFormatting (1027-1033). No scope predicate is applied. The canonical scoped pipeline DOES filter: stage1-candidate-gen.ts:1072-1092 builds scopeFilter={tenantId,userId,agentId} and calls filterRowsByScope(candidates, scopeFilter) (also re-applied at 1140, 1207, 1262, 1348). Per the pipeline's own P0 comment (stage1-candidate-gen.ts:1069-1071), sessionId is NOT a governance boundary — only tenantId/userId/agentId are. normalizedScope {tenantId,userId,agentId,sessionId} is already in scope at memory-search.ts:687, and the file already imports from scope-governance.js at line 76. The community SELECT does not even select tenant_id/user_id/agent_id columns, so a post-filter would have nothing to match on today.
findings.fix: In memory-search.ts: (1) extend the community SELECT (lines 1007-1008) to also select `tenant_id, user_id, agent_id`; (2) add `filterRowsByScope` to the existing `import { normalizeScopeContext } from '../lib/governance/scope-governance.js'` at line 76; (3) only when governance scope is present (Boolean(normalizedScope.tenantId || normalizedScope.userId || normalizedScope.agentId)), wrap memberRows with `filterRowsByScope(memberRows, { tenantId: normalizedScope.tenantId, userId: normalizedScope.userId, agentId: normalizedScope.agentId })` BEFORE the calibratedScore mapping at line 1021. Do NOT include sessionId in the scope object (mirror pipeline P0 behavior exactly). Keep the unscoped behavior identical when no governance scope is set, so single-user stdio is unaffected.
findings.verification: Add/extend a test in .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/community-search.vitest.ts: seed two memory_index rows in the same community with different tenant_id values, invoke memory_search with retrievalLevel='global', a weak query, and a tenantId scope; assert the fallback returns only the same-tenant row. Run: npx vitest run .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/community-search.vitest.ts
findings.risk: Schema field note for cluster D: no new schema field needed — tenantId/userId/agentId already exist on the memory_search input schema. Coupling: the scope-filter helper and the {tenantId,userId,agentId} (NOT sessionId) contract must stay identical to stage1-candidate-gen.ts:1072-1092; if cluster B5's sessionId hardening changes that contract, sequence B1 after it. filterRowsByScope returns [] when scope is set but rows lack matching columns, so step (1) extending the SELECT is mandatory or all fallback rows would be dropped under scope.

findings.title: Causal-graph tools operate on bare IDs without governed scope authorization
findings.currentLocation: handleMemoryDriftWhy at .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/causal-graph.ts:469-687 (memory_index reads at 560-588); handleMemoryCausalLink at 689-793
findings.evidence: handleMemoryDriftWhy (line 469) accepts only { memoryId, maxDepth, direction, relations, includeMemoryDetails } (interface DriftWhyArgs at 76-82) — no tenantId/userId/agentId. It traverses causal chains for any memoryId and reads memory_index rows via `SELECT ... FROM memory_index WHERE id = ? OR CAST(id AS TEXT) = ?` (lines 560-565 and 580-584) with no scope predicate. handleMemoryCausalLink (interface CausalLinkArgs at 84-90) takes only { sourceId, targetId, relation, strength, evidence } and inserts an edge (line 757) without verifying the caller is scoped to those memory IDs. Neither handler imports normalizeScopeContext/filterRowsByScope nor calls resolveTrustedSession. Contrast: memory-context.ts:1112 and memory-triggers.ts:228 both derive trusted scope from server context.
findings.fix: Minimal fail-closed post-filter, calibrated for single-user stdio (do NOT build auth): in handleMemoryDriftWhy, after fetching sourceMemory (line 560-565) and when an explicit governance scope is supplied, verify the source memory row matches scope via createScopeFilterPredicate({tenantId,userId,agentId}); if it does not match, return createMCPEmptyResponse (same shape as the existing total_edges===0 branch at 612-625) instead of leaking the chain. Apply the same predicate to relatedMemories rows (lines 580-588) before adding to the map. For handleMemoryCausalLink, when scope is supplied, confirm both source and target memory_index rows exist AND match scope before insertEdge (line 757); reject with createMCPErrorResponse otherwise. findings.title: Causal edge writers can create wrong-target/orphan edges (FK existence validation deferred for synthetic test IDs leaks into the production handler path)
findings.currentLocation: insertEdge FK-deferral comment at .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/causal-edges.ts:279-280; production call at handlers/causal-graph.ts:757
findings.evidence: causal-edges.ts:279-280 states `// FK check deferred — test environments use synthetic IDs not in memory_index. // Implementing FK validation would require seeding memory_index in 20+ causal edge tests.` insertEdge (253-262, body to ~400) only guards self-loops (275-277), strength clamping, and auto-edge bounds — it never checks that source_id/target_id exist in memory_index. The production handler handleMemoryCausalLink (causal-graph.ts:757) calls insertEdge(String(sourceId), String(targetId), ...) with createdBy defaulting to 'manual' and no prior existence check (the only validation at 701-755 is null/relation-type). The codebase already has the orphan-detection pattern (`NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM memory_index m WHERE CAST(m.id AS TEXT) = ce.source_id/target_id)` at causal-edges.ts:818-819, used by findOrphanedEdges), so an orphan edge created here is a known-bad state that has to be swept later.
findings.fix: Scope-locked: add FK existence validation at the PRODUCTION handler boundary (handleMemoryCausalLink in causal-graph.ts), NOT inside the shared insertEdge — this keeps the 20+ synthetic-ID unit tests passing. Between the relation-validation block (ends line 755) and the insertEdge call (line 757), add a guarded existence check: `SELECT 1 FROM memory_index WHERE id = ? OR CAST(id AS TEXT) = ?` for both String(sourceId) and String(targetId); if either is missing, return createMCPErrorResponse({ tool: 'memory_causal_link', code: ErrorCodes.CAUSAL_GRAPH_ERROR (or a dedicated not-found code if D adds one), error: 'sourceId/targetId not found in memory_index', details: { sourceId, targetId } }). Do not modify insertEdge or its deferral comment. Preserve all existing behavior for valid IDs.

(lines 83-85) but ~20 other tests use unseeded synthetic IDs — a check inside insertEdge would break them (the deferral comment documents exactly this). findings.title: memory_search does not validate caller sessionId via resolveTrustedSession like memory_context/memory_match_triggers do
findings.currentLocation: sessionId destructured at .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts:664; used raw at 604-614, 970, 1283-1296, 1301+
findings.evidence: memory-search.ts destructures the caller-supplied sessionId (line 664) and uses it directly for session-state dedup (deduplicateWithSessionState(existingResults, sessionId) at 1291), session-manager dedup (sessionManager.filterSearchResults/markResultsSent at 611-614, gated only by `sessionId && enableDedup && sessionManager.isEnabled()` at 604/1301), and as the progressiveScopeKey component (687-693, 970). It never calls sessionManager.resolveTrustedSession. By contrast memory-triggers.ts:226-246 explicitly validates rawSessionId through resolveTrustedSession with a `// SECURITY: ... prevent IDOR` comment and rejects untrusted ids, and memory-context.ts:1112 does the same. So memory_search lets a caller pass an arbitrary sessionId and read/mutate another session's sent-memory dedup state (markResultsSent writes session_sent_memories keyed by session_id).
findings.fix: Mirror the memory-triggers.ts:226-246 pattern. After destructuring (line 664) and before sessionId is first consumed for dedup/boost, when sessionId is provided, call sessionManager.resolveTrustedSession(sessionId, { tenantId, userId, agentId }); if trustedSession.error, return createMCPErrorResponse({ tool: 'memory_search', code: 'E_SESSION_SCOPE', error: trustedSession.error, recovery: { hint: 'Omit sessionId to start a new server-generated session, or reuse the effectiveSessionId returned by memory_context.' } }); otherwise replace the local sessionId with trustedSession.effectiveSessionId so all downstream dedup/boost/progressiveScopeKey use the validated id. Keep behavior identical when sessionId is omitted (current callers that omit it are unaffected). sessionManager is already imported (memory-search.ts:10).
findings.verification: Add a test in .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/gate-d-regression-session-dedup.vitest.ts (or memory-search-integration.vitest.ts): call memory_search with an untracked/forged sessionId and assert an E_SESSION_SCOPE error; call with a server-minted trusted sessionId and assert dedup still works. Run: npx vitest run .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/gate-d-regression-session-dedup.vitest.ts .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/memory-search-integration.vitest.ts
findings.risk: Behavior change for callers currently passing an arbitrary (untracked) sessionId: they will now get an error instead of silently sharing/forging dedup state. That is the intended fail-closed hardening, but verify no internal caller (hooks, bootstrap) passes a non-server-minted sessionId to memory_search — grep callers before shipping. Coupling with B5: both concern session-id trust; resolveTrustedSession mints a fresh UUID for null sessionId (session-manager.ts:421-427), which is the correct no-session behavior memory_search should keep (do NOT collapse to a process-wide id here).

findings.title: No-session callers get collapsed onto one shared process-wide session (memory_context replaces freshly-minted per-call session id with a process-wide id)
findings.evidence: resolveTrustedSession returns a fresh crypto.randomUUID() as effectiveSessionId when no sessionId is supplied (session-manager.ts:421-427, trusted:false). But memory-context.ts:1128-1131 discards that per-call UUID: `const effectiveSessionId = requestedSessionId ? trustedSession.effectiveSessionId : (process.env.SPECKIT_MEMORY_SESSION_ID?.trim() || PROCESS_MEMORY_SESSION_ID)`. PROCESS_MEMORY_SESSION_ID (164-167) is a deterministic sha256 of cwd:pid:ppid — a single value per process. This effectiveSessionId then drives session inferred-mode and event counters (1132-1133), session-state persistence (1634), working-memory prompt context (1717), and sent-memory dedup (1879). So every no-session caller in one process shares one session bucket: their inferred mode, dedup state, and working memory cross-contaminate.
findings.fix: Calibrated for single-user stdio (per cluster note, do NOT introduce per-request identity infra): the safest minimal change is to keep the process-wide id ONLY as the continuity anchor it was designed to be, but stop letting it collapse genuinely distinct concurrent callers. Minimal option that preserves current single-user continuity: leave behavior as-is when SPECKIT_MEMORY_SESSION_ID is set or no scope is provided, but when an explicit governance scope (tenantId/userId/agentId) IS provided without a sessionId, derive the no-session anchor from that scope (e.g. include the normalized scope in the hash) instead of the bare process id, so two scoped callers do not share one session bucket. Do NOT replace PROCESS_MEMORY_SESSION_ID with trustedSession.effectiveSessionId unconditionally — that would break the intended stable resume continuity for the hookless single-user case. Document the trade-off in the existing comment at 1111 (do not add packet/ADR ids).
findings.verification: Add a test in .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/session-lifecycle.vitest.ts: invoke the lifecycle resolver twice with no sessionId but two distinct {userId} scopes and assert the resulting effectiveSessionId values differ; invoke twice with identical/no scope and assert they stay stable (continuity preserved). Run: npx vitest run .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/session-lifecycle.vitest.ts .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/session-manager.vitest.ts
findings.risk: Highest-judgment finding: PROCESS_MEMORY_SESSION_ID is intentional design for hookless single-user stdio continuity (stable resume across calls), so an over-aggressive fix REGRESSES legitimate continuity. Keep the change scope-locked to the explicitly-scoped multi-caller case. Strong coupling with B4 (both about session trust) and with B1 (B1 deliberately excludes sessionId from the scope filter per stage1-candidate-gen.ts:1069 — do not let a B5 change reintroduce sessionId as a retrieval governance boundary). Sequence B4 then B5. Confirm no test asserts the literal PROCESS_MEMORY_SESSION_ID value before changing its derivation.

result.notes:
All 5 findings CONFIRMED against the current working tree; line numbers had drifted from the cited values and are corrected above. Verified evidence, not cited lines.

Schema work to hand to cluster D (I did not touch any schema files): (a) B2 needs optional tenantId/userId/agentId added to the memory_drift_why and memory_causal_link input schemas (interfaces DriftWhyArgs/CausalLinkArgs at causal-graph.ts:76-90 plus tool-input-schemas) — without them the B2 post-filter is dead code for real MCP traffic. (b) B3 may want a dedicated not-found error code (currently would reuse ErrorCodes.CAUSAL_GRAPH_ERROR). (c) B1/B4 need no new schema fields — tenantId/userId/agentId/sessionId already exist on memory_search.

Canonical scope contract to respect everywhere in this cluster: the retrieval governance boundary is {tenantId, userId, agentId} ONLY — sessionId is explicitly NOT a governance boundary (stage1-candidate-gen.ts:1069-1071 P0 comment). B1's fallback post-filter and any B5 change must honor this; sessionId is for dedup/state/continuity, not row-level access control.

Sequencing: do B2 and B3 as one coordinated edit to handleMemoryCausalLink (both land at causal-graph.ts ~755-757). Do B4 before B5 (both about session trust). B5 is the highest-judgment item — PROCESS_MEMORY_SESSION_ID is intentional single-user-stdio continuity design; the fix must be scope-locked to the explicitly-scoped multi-caller case to avoid regressing legitimate resume continuity. Per the cluster calibration note, all fixes apply a minimal fail-closed scope/post-filter and must NOT be expanded into a full multi-tenant auth system.

Existing verification anchors found: tests/community-search.vitest.ts (B1), tests/handler-causal-graph.vitest.ts + tests/integration-causal-graph.vitest.ts (B2/B3), tests/causal-edges-unit.vitest.ts (B3 regression — seeds memory_index, exercises findOrphanedEdges), tests/gate-d-regression-session-dedup.vitest.ts + tests/memory-search-integration.vitest.ts (B4), tests/session-lifecycle.vitest.ts + tests/session-manager.vitest.ts (B5).

findings.title: Entity-density cache not invalidated after memory_update (stale graph-channel routing until 60s TTL/restart)
findings.currentLocation: Shared hook missing invalidation: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/mutation-hooks.ts:20-106 (runPostMutationHooks clears trigger/tool/constitutional/graphSignals/degree/coactivation, NOT entity-density). Update path that relies on it: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-crud-update.ts:306 (runPostMutationHooks('update',...)). Underlying writer that mutates title/trigger_phrases without invalidation: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/vector-index-mutations.ts:489-495 (update_memory tx writes title + trigger_phrases). Cache + invalidator: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/search/entity-density.ts:78-103 (buildIndex reads title + trigger_phrases) and :159-163 (invalidateEntityDensityCache).
findings.evidence: mutation-hooks.ts runPostMutationHooks does NOT import or call invalidateEntityDensityCache — only triggerMatcher.clearCache, toolCache.invalidateOnWrite, clearConstitutionalCache, clearGraphSignalsCache, clearDegreeCache, clearRelatedCache. grep for invalidateEntityDensityCache across the server shows callers ONLY in: relation-backfill.ts:581, vector-index-mutations.ts:702 (delete_memory_from_database) + :859 (delete_memories), memory-save.ts:2703/2854 (save), memory-bulk-delete.ts:151/258. The memory_update handler (memory-crud-update.ts) invalidates BM25, degree, trigger, constitutional, graph caches via the transaction + runPostMutationHooks('update'), but entity-density is never touched. update_memory (vector-index-mutations.ts:489-495) directly UPDATEs title and trigger_phrases — the exact two columns buildIndex (entity-density.ts:80-100) reads to build the high-degree token set. Net effect: after a memory_update that renames a high-degree memory or rewrites its trigger phrases, getEntityDensityScore keeps matching old tokens / missing new ones until the 60s CACHE_TTL_MS lapses (entity-density.ts:22,112). entity-density.ts:155-157 docstring even enumerates current invalidation entry points (save + bulk-delete) and omits update, corroborating the gap. The existing test tests/integration/entity-density-commit-hooks.vitest.ts covers save (REQ-T1-001) and bulk-delete (REQ-T1-002) invalidation but has NO update-path case.
findings.fix: Wire entity-density invalidation into the shared post-mutation hook so it cannot be forgotten on any path. In mutation-hooks.ts: add import { invalidateEntityDensityCache } from '../lib/search/entity-density.js'; add a new guarded block inside runPostMutationHooks (mirroring the existing try/catch blocks) that calls invalidateEntityDensityCache() and records a boolean (e.g. entityDensityCacheCleared) into the returned MutationHookResult. Add the corresponding optional field to MutationHookResult in memory-crud-types.ts and surface it in buildMutationHookFeedback if that fn enumerates fields. This single addition fixes the update path (memory-crud-update.ts:306 already calls runPostMutationHooks('update')) and also covers atomic-save (memory-save.ts:3646 runPostMutationHooks('atomic-save')). Do NOT remove the existing redundant invalidations in vector-index-mutations.ts:702/859 or memory-save.ts in the same change — leave them (they are at a lower layer and harmless); removing them is out of scope and risks the delete/save fast paths. Keep comment hygiene: explain WHY (entity-density routing must reflect title/trigger_phrase mutations immediately) with no spec/packet/ADR ids.
findings.verification: Add an update-path case to .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/integration/entity-density-commit-hooks.vitest.ts: seed a high-degree memory, assert getEntityDensityScore(oldToken)>0, call handleMemoryUpdate to rename title / replace triggerPhrases, then assert getEntityDensityScore(oldToken)===0 (or new token >0) WITHOUT a TTL wait. Run: npx vitest run tests/integration/entity-density-commit-hooks.vitest.ts (from the mcp_server dir). Also run tsc/typecheck for the MutationHookResult field addition.
findings.risk: Coupling: MutationHookResult is shared by memory-crud-update.ts, memory-crud-delete.ts, memory-save.ts (atomic-save), and buildMutationHookFeedback (hooks/mutation-feedback.ts) — adding a field must be optional or all consumers updated, else typecheck breaks. Side effect: atomic-save and delete will now invalidate entity-density via the shared hook in addition to their existing lower-layer calls — double invalidation is idempotent (invalidateEntityDensityCache just resets to empty/stale) so behavior is preserved. Sequence after C2 is not required; C1 and C2 touch disjoint files except both are within the same handler tree. memory-crud-types.ts is shared with the delete handler cluster — coordinate if another cluster also edits the MutationHookResult type.

findings.title: Atomic save commits the DB row before promoting the pending file; hard-crash window leaves an unpromoted .pending file (mitigation exists + wired, but the uuid-suffixed orphan shape is untested)
findings.currentLocation: Ordering: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/save/atomic-index-memory.ts:360 (writePendingFile) -> :362 (indexPrepared = DB commit) -> :378 (promotePendingFile = rename). Wiring of those callbacks: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-save.ts:3581-3630 (indexPrepared/processPreparedMemory, getPendingPath with .<uuid8> at :3618, promotePendingFile renameSync at :3627-3629). Mitigation: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/storage/transaction-manager.ts:364-431 (recoverPendingFile/recoverAllPendingFiles) and the doubly-suffixed path parser :101-136 (parsePendingPath + PENDING_UNIQUE_SUFFIX_PATTERN at :17). Startup wiring: .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/context-server.ts:1386-1431 (recoverPendingFiles with isCommittedInDb) called from startupScan at :1442. Second (non-atomic) DB-before-file instance: memory-save.ts:2639 (writeTransaction.immediate) then :2653-2659 (finalizeMemoryFileContent after commit, explicitly warns 'DB row committed — manual file recovery may be needed').
findings.evidence: The defect described (DB indexed before file promoted) is REAL: atomic-index-memory.ts runs dependencies.indexPrepared (DB insert/commit inside processPreparedMemory) at line 362, and only renames the pending file at line 378. A SIGKILL/power-loss between commit and rename leaves a committed memory_index row whose file_path target still holds old content (or no file), with new content orphaned at the .pending.<uuid8> path. HOWEVER the crash-window mitigation is already implemented AND wired: transaction-manager.ts:283-284 documents 'recoverAllPendingFiles() can be called on startup'; context-server.ts:1442 startupScan -> recoverPendingFiles -> recoverAllPendingFiles(location, isCommittedInDb) at :1403. For the C2 scenario isCommittedInDb(originalPath) returns true (the row WAS committed), so committedInDb!==false and recoverPendingFile renames pending->final (transaction-manager.ts:409). parsePendingPath (:118-132) correctly decodes the atomic-save double-suffix foo_pending.md.a1b2c3d4 because PENDING_UNIQUE_SUFFIX_PATTERN (:17) accepts an 8-hex slice (matching randomUUID().slice(0,8) used at memory-save.ts:3618). Tests transaction-manager-recovery.vitest.ts T011-R2 (:163) prove committed-row rename works. THE GAP: every recovery test (transaction-manager.vitest.ts, transaction-manager-recovery.vitest.ts) constructs pending files via plain getPendingPath() (single _pending suffix) — none exercise the uuid-suffixed atomic-save orphan shape through findPendingFiles/recoverAllPendingFiles end-to-end, so the exact orphan form C2 produces is not regression-protected.
findings.fix: Do NOT reorder the DB-commit/promote sequence — true two-phase commit across SQLite + filesystem is not achievable and the audit's reorder option would just swap which artifact is orphaned (a promoted file with no DB row). The minimal durable fix is to lock in the already-implemented startup-reconciliation path with a regression test for the uuid-suffixed orphan, since that is the only unprotected link in the chain. Add a failure-injection test (see verification) asserting that a foo_pending.md.<uuid8> file plus a committed DB row is renamed to foo.md by recoverAllPendingFiles. No source change is required for correctness; if any source change is wanted, it is limited to a clarifying WHY-comment near atomic-index-memory.ts:362-378 noting that startup recovery (recoverAllPendingFiles) closes this window — with strict comment hygiene (no spec/packet/ADR ids).
findings.verification: Add to transaction-manager-recovery.vitest.ts: write an orphan at `${getPendingPath(original)}.deadbeef` (8-hex unique suffix) with new content, omit the original file (simulating a never-finalized new record) and provide an original file with stale content for the supersede case; call recoverAllPendingFiles(dir, () => true) and assert the orphan is renamed to `original` and its content matches the pending content. Also assert findPendingFiles(dir) includes the uuid-suffixed orphan and isPendingFile returns true for it. Run: npx vitest run tests/transaction-manager-recovery.vitest.ts tests/transaction-manager.vitest.ts. Optionally add a context-server startup test asserting recoverPendingFiles renames a uuid-suffixed orphan when isCommittedInDb is true.
findings.risk: Low. Test-only change in a file already owned by the recovery test suite; no production behavior change. Coupling: parsePendingPath/PENDING_UNIQUE_SUFFIX_PATTERN are shared by the atomic-save wiring (memory-save.ts:3618) and recovery — if any other cluster edits the suffix format, this test must track it. Note the SECOND DB-before-file instance at memory-save.ts:2653-2659 (non-atomic quality-loop finalize) is OUTSIDE the atomic pending/recovery mechanism and is NOT covered by recoverAllPendingFiles — flag for sequencing if the cluster owner wants full coverage, but it is out of the stated C2 scope (cited lines are the atomic path).

result.notes: Both findings verified by reading current code in the working tree (line numbers were close to those cited; no major drift). C1 is CONFIRMED and tightly scoped to the memory_update path: runPostMutationHooks (mutation-hooks.ts) is the shared hook every mutation handler calls, and it is the single place still missing invalidateEntityDensityCache — wiring it there (per cluster guidance) fixes update and durably prevents future omissions; delete and save already invalidate at lower layers. C2 is downgraded from a clean defect to PARTIAL: the DB-commit-before-file-rename ordering is real (atomic-index-memory.ts:362 before :378), but the crash-window mitigation (recoverAllPendingFiles with isCommittedInDb) is already implemented in transaction-manager.ts AND wired into startupScan (context-server.ts:1442), and parsePendingPath correctly handles the atomic-save uuid-suffixed pending shape. The only genuine residual gap is missing regression-test coverage for the uuid-suffixed orphan recovery path; reordering the commit/promote sequence is NOT advisable (no real 2PC across SQLite+FS). One extra observation for the cluster owner: a second, structurally similar DB-before-file write exists at memory-save.ts:2653-2659 (non-atomic quality-loop finalize) which is NOT protected by the pending-file recovery mechanism and self-documents 'manual file recovery may be needed' — out of the cited C2 scope but worth tracking.

findings.title: memory_embedding_reconcile success-coverage dry-run undercounts vs apply: dry-run counts only rows missing the rowid marker; apply repairs rows missing rowid OR the active-dim vector row
findings.evidence: computeSuccessCoverage (281-287) counts `success` rows with `NOT EXISTS (vec_memories_rowids r WHERE r.rowid = m.id)` ONLY — it never checks the active-dim vector table (the `_dimTable` param is unused, prefixed `_`). The apply repair UPDATE (417-421) uses `NOT EXISTS rowid OR NOT EXISTS ${dimTable}`. So a `success` row that HAS the rowid marker but is MISSING the dim vector row is repaired by apply (counted in applied.successCoverageReset) yet never counted in dry-run coverage.successMissingActiveVector. Line 362 derives the plannedMutations `repair_success_missing_active_vector_to_retry` row count from coverage.successMissingActiveVector — so dry-run plannedMutations under-reports what apply will mutate. The existing test tests/vector-coverage-hygiene.vitest.ts proves the dry-run gap (id=3: hasVec768=false,hasVecMemories=true is excluded from the count=2 at line 100), but its apply assertions (lines 100-104) only use id=1/id=2 where the two predicates happen to agree, so the divergence is currently untested for apply.
findings.fix: Make computeSuccessCoverage's predicate match the apply repair exactly. In embedding-reconcile.ts:282-286 change the COUNT query to include the dim-table surface: `WHERE m.embedding_status = 'success' AND (NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ${ACTIVE_SCHEMA}.vec_memories_rowids r WHERE r.rowid = m.id) OR NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ${ACTIVE_SCHEMA}.${_dimTable} v WHERE v.id = m.id))`, and rename the now-used param from `_dimTable` to `dimTable`. This aligns the dry-run count, the coverage diagnostic, and plannedMutations[362] with the apply UPDATE so dry-run truthfully predicts apply.
findings.verification: Run `vitest run tests/vector-coverage-hygiene.vitest.ts` from .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server. Add a case: insert a success row with hasVec768=false, hasVecMemories=true, assert dry-run coverage.successMissingActiveVector counts it AND an apply with repairSuccessCoverage:true resets exactly that row (applied.successCoverageReset matches the dry-run count). The existing first test's expectation `toBe(2)` must be updated to `toBe(3)` if id=3 is kept, since id=3 (has rowids, missing vec768) becomes countable.
findings.risk: Changing the existing test's expected count (2 -> 3 for the current fixture) is required and must be done deliberately, not silently. Behavior of the apply UPDATE is unchanged; only the dry-run count converges to it. Low coupling with other D findings. The doc comment block at 276-280 references the rowids table as 'the vector-presence source' — update the comment WHY only (no spec/packet ids) to reflect the dual-surface check.

findings.title: memory_embedding_reconcile contract drift across public schema / Zod / handler / docs; activeOnly accepted but never read
findings.currentLocation: tool-schemas.ts:337-353 (public memoryEmbeddingReconcile inputSchema), schemas/tool-input-schemas.ts:306-314 (Zod memoryEmbeddingReconcileSchema), :583 (ALLOWED_PARAMETERS), tools/types.ts:152-162 (ReconcileArgs), lib/embedders/embedding-reconcile.ts:19-39 (EmbeddingReconcileArgs), :22 (activeOnly declared)
findings.evidence: The schema/Zod/allow-list/type drift described in the original finding is ALREADY_FIXED: all four sources now agree on the same 8 fields {mode, activeOnly, resetMissing, missingFailureScope, maskedFailedPolicy, providerFailurePolicy, requireActiveShard, repairSuccessCoverage}. Do NOT add new runtime branching unless product intent requires activeOnly:false to skip verification (which would weaken the fail-closed safety model in embedding-reconcile.ts:8-11). findings.verification: Run `vitest run tests/tool-input-schema.vitest.ts` to confirm schema still validates. Confirm parity via the D7 test (which would now pass for this tool since all 4 sources agree). For the activeOnly no-op: `grep -rn 'args.activeOnly\|\.activeOnly' lib/embedders handlers/memory-embedding-reconcile.ts` returns nothing, proving it is unread.
findings.risk: Low. The schema/Zod/type alignment is already done — do NOT re-do it. Only the activeOnly dead-param remains. Avoid scope creep into changing the safety model. Note: tool-schemas.ts:346 contains '[Phase 007]' inside a JSON `description` string (data, not a code comment) — leave it; comment-hygiene applies to code comments, not advertised schema description text, but flag separately if doc-cluster wants it cleaned.

findings.title: Governed ingest metadata (provenance/retention/deleteAfter + scope tuple) validated then dropped before indexing on scan and async-ingest paths
findings.currentLocation: handlers/memory-index.ts:330-332 (validateGovernedIngest gate-only), :287-292 (indexSingleFile forwards no governance), :721-725 (scan loop call); handlers/memory-ingest.ts:146-148 (validate), :260-281 (createIngestJob called with only {id,paths,specFolder}); lib/ops/job-queue.ts:45-55 (IngestJob has no governance fields), :253-295 (createIngestJob persists only spec_folder), :626-627 (worker calls processFileFn(nextPath) with no metadata); handlers/memory-save.ts:2745-2777 (IndexMemoryFileOptions lacks provenance/retention/deleteAfter)
findings.evidence: Both bulk paths validate governance then discard it. SCAN path: memory-index.ts:330 calls validateGovernedIngest(args) but only uses governanceDecision.allowed as a boolean gate (331-332); the normalized provenance/retention/deleteAfter is never read. indexSingleFile (287-292) forwards only {force, qualityGateMode, fromScan, asyncEmbedding} to indexMemoryFile, and the scan loop at 721-725 passes no governance. IndexMemoryFileOptions (memory-save.ts:2745-2763) accepts `scope` (tenant/user/agent/session) but NOT provenanceSource/provenanceActor/governedAt/retentionPolicy/deleteAfter, and the scan path doesn't even set `scope`. INGEST/async path: memory-ingest.ts:146 validates, then createIngestJob (ingest call at 260-263) is invoked with only {id, paths, specFolder}. job-queue.ts has ZERO references to provenance/retention/governance — IngestJob interface (45-55) and the ingest_jobs table INSERT (263-281) persist only spec_folder, so the worker (626-627) re-indexes with no governance context. By contrast the direct handleMemorySave path DOES thread governanceDecision.normalized (memory-save.ts:2972-2976, 3056-3060, 3446) — proving the propagation exists but is absent on bulk/scan/async. findings.title: Bootstrap/stale-graph guidance claims code_graph_query still works on a stale graph, but the code-graph handler blocks every non-fresh read

findings.title: RC1 structural fix: no single source of truth / no parity test guarding public JSON schema vs Zod allow-list vs handler args

findings.title: ROOT: deriveStatus() ignores spec.md metadata-table Status, marking Draft/Placeholder specs 'complete' from implementation-summary presence
findings.currentLocation: graph-metadata-parser.ts:623 (collectPacketDocs sets doc.status = normalizeDerivedStatus(extractFrontmatterScalar(content,'status')) — frontmatter ONLY); :987-1038 deriveStatus; the offending branch is :1030-1033 (no checklist.md -> return 'complete') and :1012-1015 (frontmatterStatus never populated for table-only specs). extractFrontmatterScalar:436-449 calls extractFrontmatter first so it never reads the markdown table.
findings.evidence: Verified: extractFrontmatterScalar only parses YAML frontmatter. Real 027 specs (003/006) and many 026 specs declare status ONLY in the `| **Status** | Draft/In Progress |` metadata table, with NO YAML `status:` key. Therefore doc.status is null for spec.md, frontmatterStatus (deriveStatus:1012) is empty, and the heuristic falls to :1030-1033: when implementation-summary.md exists and checklist.md is absent, it returns 'complete' — overriding a Draft/Placeholder spec. No table-status parser exists anywhere in the parser (grep confirmed). The cited generate-context.ts:455 is now updatePhaseParentPointer (STALE line) — the status heuristic lives solely in graph-metadata-parser.ts; generate-context.ts only orchestrates and does not re-derive status. backfill-graph-metadata.ts:159-193 (collectReviewFlags/runBackfill) calls deriveGraphMetadata and inherits the same bug.
findings.fix: Add a metadata-table status fallback that is consulted with HIGHER precedence than the implementation-summary/checklist heuristic. Minimal: add a helper extractMetadataTableStatus(content) that matches `^\|\s*\**\s*Status\s*\**\s*\|\s*([^|]+?)\s*\|` (case-insensitive, multiline) and normalizes via normalizeDerivedStatus; in collectPacketDocs:623 set status = frontmatter status ?? table status. Critically, a spec with table Status 'Draft'/'Placeholder' must yield a non-complete derived status: ensure normalizeDerivedStatus maps 'draft'/'placeholder' to themselves (they already pass through default branch) and that deriveStatus returns the spec.md table status before reaching the :1030 'complete' branch. Do NOT broaden scope to redesign the whole precedence chain — only inject the table-status read into the existing frontmatterStatus path (deriveStatus ranked list at :1006-1011 already reads doc.status for spec.md, so populating spec.md doc.status from the table is the single load-bearing change). No spec-path/ADR/packet ids in comments.
findings.verification: Existing: `npx vitest run .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/tests/graph-metadata-schema.vitest.ts .opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/scripts/tests/graph-metadata-refresh.vitest.ts` must stay green (they use YAML status frontmatter, unaffected). NEW test: add a fixture spec.md with NO YAML status but `| **Status** | Draft |` table row plus an implementation-summary.md and no checklist, assert deriveGraphMetadata(...).derived.status === 'draft' (not 'complete'); and a 'Placeholder' variant. Then re-run backfill dry-run over 026/027 and confirm the 163 reported contradictions collapse.
findings.risk: HIGHEST coupling node — E3/E6 data fixes are non-durable until this lands, and a backfill regen after this will REWRITE derived.status across 026/027 (the '163 contradictions'). That mass rewrite is the intended remediation but must be reviewed: a too-greedy table regex could capytalize wrong rows or mis-handle phase-parents (which legitimately have no status table). Guard: only override when the table status normalizes to a known/explicit value; keep the lean-phase-parent existingStatus preservation at :1027 intact (graph-metadata-refresh.vitest.ts asserts it). compiled dist must be rebuilt (parser is TS in mcp_server) before backfill uses it. Sequence FIRST in the cluster.

findings.title: sk-doc command requirements split across three incompatible contracts

Update quick_reference.md lines 125-129 and core_standards.md lines 113-117 to match template_rules.json: required = [purpose, instructions], recommended = [contract, examples, notes]. Remove 'INPUTS, WORKFLOW, OUTPUTS' from the required list in core_standards.md.

findings.title: comment-hygiene checker misses several EXACT forbidden examples that the constitutional rule lists
findings.evidence: Tested all forbidden examples from comment-hygiene.md table against current VIOLATION_PATTERNS. MISSED: (1) bare spec path '.opencode/specs/012-foo/' — current pattern `specs/[a-z0-9-]+/\d{3}-` requires a sub-path segment so a top-level spec-folder path with no child is not caught; (2) 'REQ-042' without trailing dash/colon — pattern is `\bREQ-\d+[-:]` so bare REQ-042 is missed; (3) 'CHK-007' without trailing dash/colon — same issue; (4) bare 'T042' — pattern is `\bT\d{3,4}[-/]` requiring suffix; (5) 'checklist item 3' — no pattern; (6) 'P1-finding-2' — only 'review finding' phrase is caught, not Pn-finding-N form; (7) 'finding #7' — not caught.
findings.fix: In check-comment-hygiene.sh VIOLATION_PATTERNS (lines 85-97), make the following targeted changes: (a) Change REQ pattern from `\bREQ-\d+[-:]` to `\bREQ-\d+\b` to catch bare REQ-042. (b) Change CHK pattern from `\bCHK-\d+[-:]` to `\bCHK-\d+\b`. (c) Change T-id pattern from `\bT\d{3,4}[-/]` to `\bT\d{3,4}\b` (note: verify this does not false-positive on variable names like T0 or common abbreviations — the 3-4 digit constraint provides reasonable specificity). (d) Add pattern `(re.compile(r'\bchecklist\s+item\s+\d+\b', re.IGNORECASE), 'checklist item reference')`. (e) Add pattern `(re.compile(r'\b[Pp]\d-finding[-\s]\d+\b'), 'finding id reference')`. (f) Add pattern `(re.compile(r'\bfinding\s+#\d+\b', re.IGNORECASE), 'finding id reference')`. (g) Change spec-path pattern from `specs/[a-z0-9-]+/\d{3}-` to `specs/[a-z0-9]+-[a-z0-9-]*/` to also catch top-level spec-folder paths.
findings.verification: python3 .opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh on a scratch .ts file containing each of the missed patterns — each must exit 1. Run the existing test file if one exists: grep -r 'test.*hygiene\|hygiene.*test' .opencode/
findings.risk: Broadening regex patterns risks false-positives. The T\d{3,4} change is the highest risk — existing code with variable names like T1234 would be flagged. Consider restricting to comment-only context (already done by is_comment_line) which reduces false-positives significantly. No coupling to other clusters.

findings.title: A broad `hygiene-ok` escape can bypass an unconditional constitutional comment rule
findings.evidence: Line 12 (docstring): 'Escape: add "hygiene-ok" anywhere on a comment line to suppress it.' Lines 108-110: `if "hygiene-ok" in raw_line: continue` — this unconditionally skips the entire violation check for that line. The constitutional rule (comment-hygiene.md line 71) says 'Neither gate can be bypassed by --no-verify without an explicit user override and a documented reason.' The hygiene-ok escape requires no user override and no documented reason — a single token on a comment line silently drops enforcement for that line. This is broader than --no-verify (which blocks the whole commit); hygiene-ok operates per-line with zero audit trail.
findings.fix: The escape itself is intentional (it is documented and serves real false-positive suppression). The constitutional doc needs updating to acknowledge the per-line escape. In comment-hygiene.md line 71, change 'Neither gate can be bypassed by --no-verify without an explicit user override and a documented reason.' to 'The pre-commit gate cannot be bypassed by --no-verify. Individual lines may be exempted by appending `// hygiene-ok` — use only for documented false-positives (e.g. stable standard-body refs that trigger a pattern).'
findings.verification: Read the updated line in comment-hygiene.md and verify it accurately describes both the --no-verify protection and the hygiene-ok per-line escape.
findings.risk: Documentation-only fix. The checker behavior is unchanged. No coupling to other clusters.

findings.title: comment-hygiene enforcement is documented protecting more paths than it actually does
findings.evidence: comment-hygiene.md lines 66-69 says 'Two gates check every code comment write' listing only pre-commit and PostToolUse. In reality there are THREE enforcement surfaces: (1) claude-posttooluse.sh (warn-only, exits 0 always per line 88), (2) .opencode/hooks/pre-commit (blocking), (3) .github/workflows/comment-hygiene.yml (CI, blocking on PRs). Additionally, the constitutional doc line 71 claims 'Neither gate can be bypassed by --no-verify' but git-hooks/pre-commit line 50 shows `SPECKIT_SKIP_COMMENT_HYGIENE=1` env-var bypass is available.
findings.fix: In comment-hygiene.md, update the Enforcement section (lines 66-71): (a) Change 'Two gates' to 'Three gates'. (b) Add the CI gate as point 3: '3. CI gate — .github/workflows/comment-hygiene.yml re-validates on every PR to main; this gate cannot be bypassed with --no-verify.' (c) Update line 71 to: 'The pre-commit gate cannot be bypassed with --no-verify but can be skipped with SPECKIT_SKIP_COMMENT_HYGIENE=1 (requires explicit operator intent). The CI gate cannot be bypassed from the command line.'
findings.verification: Read the updated Enforcement section and cross-check against .opencode/hooks/pre-commit, .opencode/scripts/git-hooks/pre-commit, claude-posttooluse.sh, and comment-hygiene.yml to confirm all three gates and the bypass mechanism are accurately described.
findings.risk: Documentation-only. No code changes. No coupling to other clusters.

findings.title: Documented comment-hygiene command invocation via bash is broken — file is Python
findings.evidence: All three docs instruct: `bash .opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh <file>`. Verified: running `bash check-comment-hygiene.sh` fails immediately with 'import: command not found' and 'syntax error near unexpected token def main()' because the file is a Python3 script (shebang: #!/usr/bin/env python3) named with a .sh extension. The working invocations are `python3 .opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh <file>` or bare `.opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh <file>` (shebang-direct). The pre-commit hook at .opencode/hooks/pre-commit line 21 uses `$CHECKER_PATH` directly (no `bash` prefix) so it works correctly already.
findings.fix: In SKILL.md line 216, code_quality_standards.md line 108, and universal_checklist.md line 254, change every occurrence of `bash .opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh <file>` to `.opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh <file>` (remove the `bash` prefix; rely on the shebang).
findings.verification: After edit, run: `.opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh <any-.ts-file>` and confirm exit 0 or 1 (not a shell parse error). Verify no remaining `bash .opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene.sh` in any doc: `rg 'bash .opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts/check-comment-hygiene' .`
findings.risk: Documentation-only change. The pre-commit hook already uses the correct invocation. Changing the docs is safe and has no side effects.

findings.title: sk-code alignment verifier reports Python .sh entrypoints as shell-style drift (SH-SHEBANG + SH-STRICT-MODE)
findings.evidence: verify_alignment_drift.py line 39 maps .sh extension to 'shell', then check_shell (lines 308-333) requires first line to equal `#!/usr/bin/env bash` and looks for `set -euo pipefail`. check-comment-hygiene.sh line 1 is `#!/usr/bin/env python3` and claude-posttooluse.sh line 1 is `#!/usr/bin/env python3` — both use Python shebangs. The verifier will emit SH-SHEBANG and SH-STRICT-MODE findings (both WARN) on every scan that includes these files.
findings.fix: In verify_alignment_drift.py check_shell function (lines 308-333), add an early-exit guard: if `first_line == '#!/usr/bin/env python3'`, return an empty findings list (the file is a Python script with a .sh extension; Python shebang validity is checked by check_python). This keeps the shebang-based routing accurate without misrouting Python content.
findings.verification: Run: `python3 .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/scripts/verify_alignment_drift.py --root .opencode/skills/sk-code/scripts` — confirm no SH-SHEBANG or SH-STRICT-MODE findings for check-comment-hygiene.sh or claude-posttooluse.sh. Run the existing test suite: `python3 -m pytest .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/scripts/test_verify_alignment_drift.py -v`
findings.risk: The early-exit guard in check_shell only fires on the exact string `#!/usr/bin/env python3`. Legitimate shell scripts with bash shebangs are unaffected. If a third .sh Python file is added later it will also benefit from this guard. Couples with G8 (both involve the Python-.sh naming pattern).

findings.title: Default sk-code alignment verification can PASS with warning-only P0 checklist gaps
findings.evidence: classify_severity (lines 235-239): only rules whose rule_id starts with INTEGRITY_RULE_PREFIXES ('COMMON-', 'JSON-', 'JSONC-') get severity ERROR; all language-specific rules (SH-SHEBANG, SH-STRICT-MODE, PY-SHEBANG, JS-USE-STRICT, TS-MODULE-HEADER) get severity WARN. main() line 458: `should_fail = error_count > 0 or (args.fail_on_warn and warning_count > 0)` — without --fail-on-warn, WARNs are ignored and the tool prints PASS. But universal_checklist.md lines 20-23 defines P0 as 'HARD BLOCKER — Must fix before commit', and the checklist's P0 section (lines 28-48) explicitly lists file headers as P0, citing verify_alignment_drift.py as the verifier. The verifier thus reports PASS for P0-level violations.
findings.fix: In verify_alignment_drift.py, add language-specific shebang and module-header rules to INTEGRITY_RULE_PREFIXES, or create a dedicated set of rule IDs that are always ERROR. Minimal change: in classify_severity (line 236), extend the ERROR condition to also include rules with prefixes 'SH-', 'PY-', 'TS-MODULE', 'JS-STRICT' (the header/shebang rules that map to P0 checklist items). Specifically: `ERROR_PREFIXES = ('COMMON-', 'JSON-', 'JSONC-', 'SH-SHEBANG', 'SH-STRICT-MODE', 'PY-SHEBANG', 'TS-MODULE-HEADER')` and change `INTEGRITY_RULE_PREFIXES` to `ERROR_PREFIXES` in the startswith check.
findings.verification: Run: `python3 -m pytest .opencode/skills/sk-code/assets/scripts/test_verify_alignment_drift.py -v` — the existing test `test_warning_only_exit_code_is_zero_by_default` (line 120) checks that a JS-USE-STRICT warning exits 0. If JS-USE-STRICT becomes an ERROR that test will fail and must be updated to reflect the new intent. Also run the verifier on a temp .py file missing a shebang and confirm exit 1.
findings.risk: This change tightens enforcement and will cause existing PASS results on repos with missing headers to become FAIL. The test at test_verify_alignment_drift.py line 120 explicitly tests the old behavior and will break — it must be updated together with the verifier. Couples with G9: G9's fix (ignoring Python shebangs on .sh files) must land before or alongside G10 to avoid false failures on check-comment-hygiene.sh.

result.notes: All 10 findings verified against current working tree. Line numbers mostly match cited locations with two exceptions: G3 cites validate_document.py:466 as filename enforcement — that line is actually the apply_fixes function, not filename validation; the filename rule lives in core_standards.md:31-35 and SKILL.md:47 (both confirmed). G8 is the most immediately impactful operational defect: `bash check-comment-hygiene.sh` is the documented invocation in 3 separate docs but fails at runtime because the file is Python. The pre-commit hook already uses the correct bare invocation so the gate works — only the developer-facing docs are broken. G9 and G10 are coupled: fix G9 before G10 to avoid false-positive SH-SHEBANG errors being promoted to ERROR severity.
